news_summary: Recent breakthroughs show promising advances in brain health, heart protection, oral care, and rejuvenation through innovative treatments and nutritional discoveries.
news_summary_fr: Des avancées récentes laissent entrevoir des progrès prometteurs en matière de santé cérébrale, de protection cardiaque, d'hygiène bucco-dentaire et de rajeunissement, grâce à des traitements innovants et à des découvertes nutritionnelles.
news_summary_es: Los últimos avances muestran progresos prometedores en materia de salud cerebral, protección cardíaca, higiene bucodental y rejuvenecimiento gracias a tratamientos innovadores y descubrimientos nutricionales.

articles.title: Scientists reprogram brain immune cells to fight Alzheimer’s
articles.summary: A newly identified molecule called OLE helped restore the brain’s immune cells to a more protective state in Alzheimer’s models. The treatment reduced toxic plaque buildup and improved memory, raising hopes for a new therapeutic approach.
articles.content:
Researchers in Spain and Switzerland have identified an experimental molecule that may help restore the brain's natural defenses against Alzheimer's disease. The compound, known as OLE, appears to "reprogram" microglia, the brain's immune cells, allowing them to regain some of their protective abilities.

The research was led by José Vicente Sánchez Mut of the Institute for Neurosciences (IN), a joint center of the Spanish National Research Council (CSIC) and Miguel Hernández University of Elche (UMH), together with Johannes Gräff of the École Polytechnique Fédérale de Lausanne (EPFL). Their findings were published in the journal Cell Death and Disease.

According to the study, OLE helps microglia surround and contain beta-amyloid plaques, reducing both their size and their harmful effects. In animal studies, the treatment also led to better performance on memory tests.

How OLE Targets Alzheimer's Disease

One of the hallmarks of Alzheimer's disease is the buildup of beta-amyloid plaques in the brain. At the same time, microglia, which normally help remove these toxic deposits, gradually become less effective. As their protective functions decline, they can contribute to damage in brain cells.

The researchers found that OLE, a molecule derived from the PM20D1 gene, can shift microglia back into a more protective state. After treatment, the cells moved toward beta-amyloid plaques and surrounded them, creating a barrier that limited contact between the plaques and nearby neurons. This reduced the plaques' toxic impact on brain tissue.

"One of the most significant findings is that we have identified a molecule capable of restoring microglia's protective function," explains Sánchez Mut. "In Alzheimer's disease, these cells become progressively impaired. Our results suggest that this process can be reversed, pointing to new therapeutic and research avenues to counteract the disease," adds the researcher, who leads the Functional Epi-Genomics of Aging and Alzheimer's Disease laboratory at the IN CSIC-UMH.

Testing OLE in Worms and Mice

To evaluate the effects of OLE, the researchers used several experimental models.

The first involved genetically modified worms (C. elegans) that produce beta-amyloid. Because these worms develop disease-related damage quickly, they provide a useful way to study toxicity. Treatment with OLE reduced the buildup of protein aggregates and improved the animals' movement, indicating a protective effect.

The team then tested the compound in mouse models of Alzheimer's disease. Mice received OLE for three months, after which researchers examined both memory and brain changes. The treated animals performed better on memory tests and showed fewer beta-amyloid plaques than untreated mice.

Microglia Show the Strongest Response

To better understand how OLE works, the researchers examined the activity of thousands of individual cells in the brain. Their analysis revealed that microglia were the cells most strongly affected by the treatment.

Following exposure to OLE, microglia activated pathways involved in clearing beta-amyloid and regained their ability to move toward plaques and contain them.

"Single-cell analysis allowed us to determine that microglia were the cells that responded most strongly to the treatment," says Victoria Pozzi, first author of the study. "From there, we observed that the compound helped these cells move toward beta-amyloid plaques and better contain the damage associated with the disease," adds the researcher.

Additional experiments in cell cultures produced similar results. Microglia treated with OLE were more effective at moving toward beta-amyloid deposits and helping remove them. In separate neuronal cultures exposed to conditions resembling those seen in Alzheimer's disease, OLE improved cell survival, suggesting the compound may also directly protect neurons.

Potential for Future Alzheimer's Therapies

The findings are covered by two European patents, including one owned by the CSIC. The researchers say this strengthens the translational potential of the work and supports future efforts to develop therapeutic applications based on the discovery.

The study received funding from the Dementia Research Switzerland -- Synapsis Foundation (Switzerland), the Pasqual Maragall Researchers Programme (PMRP) of the Pasqual Maragall Foundation, the Spanish Ministry of Science, Innovation and Universities, the Severo Ochoa Centres of Excellence programme of the State Research Agency (AEI), the Prometeo program of the Generalitat Valenciana, the European Regional Development Fund (ERDF), and the CSIC Interdisciplinary Thematic Platform PTI+ NEURO-AGING. Additional support came from the Swiss National Science Foundation, the École Polytechnique Fédérale de Lausanne (EPFL), the European Research Council (ERC), the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF), and the European Social Fund (ESF+).
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough where researchers discovered a molecule (OLE) that can reprogram brain immune cells to reduce toxic plaques and improve memory in Alzheimer's models. This has broad implications for millions affected by Alzheimer's disease worldwide, offering hope for new therapeutic approaches. The article provides detailed context on the research, experimental models, and potential future applications, fulfilling the criteria for inspiring good news with substantial positive real-world impact.
articles.personality_title: New molecule helps brain cells fight Alzheimer's disease
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Alzheimer's disease causes harmful clumps called beta-amyloid plaques to build up in the brain. Normally, special immune cells in the brain called microglia help clear these plaques, but in Alzheimer's, microglia lose this ability and the plaques damage brain cells.

**What happened** – Scientists from Spain and Switzerland discovered a molecule named OLE that can restore microglia to a more protective state. In experiments with worms and mice that have Alzheimer's-like symptoms, OLE helped microglia surround and reduce plaques. Treated mice also showed improved memory.

**Impact** – This is important because it shows microglia can be 'reprogrammed' to fight Alzheimer's damage again. The molecule acts like a switch to bring back the brain’s natural defense, reducing toxic plaques and protecting brain cells. This could lead to new treatments that slow down or stop the disease.

**What's next step** – Researchers plan to develop OLE-based therapies for humans. The discovery is already protected by patents, and the team will work on tests to ensure the molecule is safe and effective in people.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists found a molecule that helps brain immune cells reduce harmful plaques and improve memory in Alzheimer's models, opening the door to new treatments.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une nouvelle molécule aide les cellules cérébrales à combattre la maladie d'Alzheimer
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La maladie d'Alzheimer provoque l'accumulation de plaques nocives appelées plaques bêta-amyloïdes dans le cerveau. Normalement, des cellules immunitaires spéciales du cerveau, appelées microglies, aident à éliminer ces plaques, mais dans la maladie d'Alzheimer, les microglies perdent cette capacité et les plaques endommagent les cellules cérébrales.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des scientifiques d'Espagne et de Suisse ont découvert une molécule nommée OLE qui peut restaurer les microglies dans un état plus protecteur. Dans des expériences sur des vers et des souris présentant des symptômes similaires à ceux de la maladie d'Alzheimer, OLE a aidé les microglies à entourer et réduire les plaques. Les souris traitées ont également montré une amélioration de la mémoire.

**Impact** – C’est important car cela montre que les microglies peuvent être « reprogrammées » pour lutter à nouveau contre les dégâts de l’Alzheimer. La molécule agit comme un interrupteur pour réactiver la défense naturelle du cerveau, réduisant les plaques toxiques et protégeant les cellules cérébrales. Cela pourrait mener à de nouveaux traitements ralentissant ou arrêtant la maladie.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs prévoient de développer des thérapies basées sur OLE pour les humains. La découverte est déjà protégée par des brevets, et l’équipe travaillera sur des tests pour garantir la sécurité et l’efficacité de la molécule chez l’homme.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Des scientifiques ont trouvé une molécule qui aide les cellules immunitaires du cerveau à réduire les plaques nocives et à améliorer la mémoire dans des modèles d’Alzheimer, ouvrant la voie à de nouveaux traitements.
articles.personality_title_es: Nueva molécula ayuda a células cerebrales a combatir el Alzheimer
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La enfermedad de Alzheimer provoca la acumulación de placas dañinas llamadas placas beta-amiloides en el cerebro. Normalmente, unas células inmunitarias especiales del cerebro, llamadas microglías, ayudan a eliminar estas placas, pero en el Alzheimer las microglías pierden esta función y las placas dañan las células cerebrales.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos de España y Suiza descubrieron una molécula llamada OLE que puede restaurar las microglías a un estado más protector. En experimentos con gusanos y ratones con síntomas similares al Alzheimer, OLE ayudó a las microglías a rodear y reducir las placas. Los ratones tratados también mostraron mejor memoria.

**Impacto** – Esto es importante porque muestra que las microglías pueden ser 'reprogramadas' para combatir nuevamente el daño del Alzheimer. La molécula actúa como un interruptor para devolver la defensa natural del cerebro, reduciendo placas tóxicas y protegiendo las células cerebrales. Esto podría llevar a nuevos tratamientos para frenar o detener la enfermedad.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores planean desarrollar terapias basadas en OLE para humanos. El descubrimiento ya está protegido por patentes y el equipo trabajará en pruebas para asegurar que la molécula sea segura y eficaz en personas.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Científicos encontraron una molécula que ayuda a las células inmunitarias del cerebro a reducir placas dañinas y mejorar la memoria en modelos de Alzheimer, abriendo la puerta a nuevos tratamientos.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of glowing, protective microglia cells shaped like gentle, luminous guardians embracing and surrounding soft, abstract clusters of beta-amyloid plaques within a stylized, softly colored brain landscape, symbolizing restored defense and healing.

articles.title: Think you're eating healthy? You may be missing this heart-protecting nutrient
articles.summary: Eating five servings of fruits and vegetables may not be enough if you're missing foods rich in flavanols, a group of compounds linked to better heart health. Researchers found that choices like blackberries, plums, apples, broad beans, cherries, and green tea can dramatically increase flavanol intake.
articles.content:
Adding foods like blueberries, plums, blackberries, broad beans, and cherries to your daily diet, especially when paired with green tea, could be a simple way to support heart health, according to new research.

A large international study led by scientists from the University of Reading, Harvard Medical School, the University of California Davis, and Mars, Inc., found that most people are not consuming enough flavanols, natural compounds linked to a lower risk of heart disease.

The researchers discovered that fewer than 20% of people reached the flavanol intake level associated with heart health benefits. Even many individuals who regularly ate the recommended five daily servings of fruits and vegetables failed to meet that target.

Published on June 8, 2026, in the journal Food and Function, the study analyzed dietary data from more than 30,000 people in the United Kingdom and the United States using biomarker measurements to assess flavanol intake.

Most People Fall Short on Flavanols

Dr. Javier Ottaviani, the study's lead author, said: "Flavanols can significantly reduce the risk of dying from cardiovascular disease, but only if you consume enough of them. Most people assume that eating plenty of fruit and vegetables covers this, but what this research shows is that the specific choices you make matter far more than the total amount. Including a handful of blackberries, a whole apple or having a cup of green tea alongside your meal could make a real difference to how much of these beneficial compounds you actually consume and absorb from the diet."

The findings suggest that simply increasing fruit and vegetable intake may not be enough. The specific foods people choose appear to play an important role in determining how many flavanols they actually consume.

Foods Highest in Heart Healthy Flavanols

Earlier research, including the COSMOS study, the largest clinical trial to examine flavanols, found that consuming 500 milligrams of flavanols per day significantly lowered the risk of death from heart disease.

The new study indicates that most people remain well below that level, even when following standard healthy eating recommendations such as the NHS Eatwell Guide.

Researchers identified the following foods as some of the richest dietary sources of flavanols per serving:

Plums (500g, roughly one punnet): ~450mg of flavanols

Cranberries (250g, roughly one punnet): ~300mg of flavanols

Blackberries (200g, roughly one punnet): ~250mg of flavanols

Green tea (one 250ml cup): ~200mg of flavanols

Broad beans/fava beans (80g, a small handful): ~140mg of flavanols

Cherries (400g, roughly one punnet): ~130mg of flavanols

Apples with skin (200g, one medium apple): ~110mg of flavanols

Strawberries (200g, roughly one punnet): ~90mg of flavanols

Blueberries (150g, roughly one punnet): ~80mg of flavanols

Pinto beans (40g, two tablespoons dry): ~70mg of flavanols

Could Dietary Guidelines Be Improved?

The results also raise questions about whether current nutrition recommendations could do a better job of helping people obtain beneficial compounds such as flavanols.

Professor Gunter Kuhnle of the University of Reading said: "Five-a-day is the right message, but we may need to think more carefully about which five. Different fruits and vegetables offer very different nutritional benefits beyond vitamins and minerals, and as our understanding of these compounds grows, there is a real opportunity to make dietary guidance more specific and more effective. This research is a step towards understanding what that might look like in practice."

The researchers say the findings highlight an important point. While eating plenty of fruits and vegetables remains a cornerstone of a healthy diet, the types of produce chosen may have a significant impact on heart health benefits.
articles.reasoning: The article reports on a large international study identifying the importance of specific flavanol-rich foods for heart health, revealing that many people do not consume enough of these compounds despite eating recommended amounts of fruits and vegetables. This insight has broad public health significance and could lead to improved dietary guidelines, potentially reducing cardiovascular disease risk on a large scale.
articles.personality_title: Certain fruits and green tea boost heart health more than others, study finds
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Eating fruits and vegetables is known to be healthy, but new research shows that not all fruits and vegetables protect the heart equally. Many people may miss out on important nutrients called flavanols, which help lower the risk of heart disease.

**What happened** – An international study involving over 30,000 people from the UK and the US found that fewer than 20% of participants ate enough flavanol-rich foods. Researchers measured flavanol levels using special tests and discovered that even those eating the recommended five servings of fruits and vegetables daily often fell short. Foods like blackberries, plums, apples, broad beans, cherries, and green tea provide the highest amounts of flavanols.

**Impact** – This study reveals that just eating fruits and vegetables is not enough for heart protection; the types of fruits and vegetables matter a lot. For example, one cup of green tea or a handful of blackberries can significantly raise flavanol intake. Knowing which foods contain more flavanols can help people make better choices to support their heart health.

**What's next step** – Experts suggest that health guidelines may need to be updated to recommend specific flavanol-rich foods instead of just a general fruit and vegetable target. This could help people get more heart benefits from their diet and reduce deaths from heart disease.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Choosing certain fruits, vegetables, and green tea rich in flavanols can better protect your heart than just eating any fruits and vegetables.
articles.personality_title_fr: Certaines fruits et le thé vert protègent mieux le cœur, selon une étude
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Manger des fruits et légumes est bon pour la santé, mais une nouvelle étude montre que tous ne protègent pas le cœur de la même façon. Beaucoup de personnes ne consomment pas assez de flavanols, des nutriments importants pour réduire le risque de maladie cardiaque.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une étude internationale portant sur plus de 30 000 personnes au Royaume-Uni et aux États-Unis a révélé que moins de 20 % des participants mangeaient suffisamment d’aliments riches en flavanols. Les chercheurs ont mesuré ces niveaux avec des tests spéciaux et ont découvert que même ceux qui mangeaient les cinq portions recommandées de fruits et légumes par jour n’atteignaient souvent pas cet apport. Les aliments comme les mûres, les prunes, les pommes, les fèves, les cerises et le thé vert contiennent le plus de flavanols.

**Impact** – Cette étude montre que manger des fruits et légumes ne suffit pas pour protéger le cœur; le choix des fruits et légumes est très important. Par exemple, une tasse de thé vert ou une poignée de mûres peut augmenter fortement l’apport en flavanols. Connaître ces aliments aide à faire de meilleurs choix pour la santé du cœur.

**Prochaine étape** – Les experts suggèrent de revoir les recommandations alimentaires pour conseiller des aliments riches en flavanols plutôt que de simplement dire de manger cinq portions de fruits et légumes. Cela pourrait aider à mieux protéger le cœur et réduire les décès par maladies cardiaques.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Choisir des fruits, légumes et thé vert riches en flavanols protège mieux le cœur que de manger n’importe quels fruits et légumes.
articles.personality_title_es: Ciertas frutas y té verde protegen mejor el corazón, según estudio
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Comer frutas y verduras es saludable, pero una nueva investigación muestra que no todas protegen igual el corazón. Muchas personas no consumen suficientes flavanoles, nutrientes que ayudan a reducir el riesgo de enfermedades del corazón.

**Qué pasó** – Un estudio internacional con más de 30,000 personas en Reino Unido y Estados Unidos encontró que menos del 20% consumía suficientes alimentos ricos en flavanoles. Los investigadores usaron pruebas especiales para medir los niveles y descubrieron que incluso quienes comían las cinco porciones diarias recomendadas de frutas y verduras, a menudo no alcanzaban el nivel necesario. Alimentos como moras, ciruelas, manzanas, habas, cerezas y té verde tienen la mayor cantidad de flavanoles.

**Impacto** – El estudio muestra que solo comer frutas y verduras no basta para proteger el corazón; importa mucho qué frutas y verduras se eligen. Por ejemplo, una taza de té verde o un puñado de moras puede aumentar mucho el consumo de flavanoles. Saber cuáles alimentos tienen más flavanoles ayuda a tomar mejores decisiones para la salud del corazón.

**Próximo paso** – Los expertos proponen actualizar las guías alimentarias para recomendar alimentos ricos en flavanoles en lugar de solo sugerir cinco porciones de frutas y verduras. Esto podría ayudar a proteger mejor el corazón y reducir muertes por enfermedades cardíacas.

**Resumen en una frase** – Elegir frutas, verduras y té verde ricos en flavanoles protege mejor el corazón que solo comer cualquier fruta o verdura.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a vibrant still life featuring ripe blueberries, blackberries, plums, cherries, broad beans, and a steaming cup of green tea arranged harmoniously on a rustic wooden table, with soft natural tones highlighting the rich colors and textures of the fruits and beans, symbolizing heart health and the power of specific nutrient-rich foods.

articles.title: This giant tropical fruit could help reverse gum disease damage
articles.summary: A new biomaterial made from jackfruit latex, pomegranate peel, and simvastatin could transform the treatment of severe gum disease. Early tests suggest it not only combats infection and inflammation but may also help rebuild lost bone and tissue around teeth.
articles.content:
Researchers in Brazil have created a new biomaterial made from jackfruit latex, pomegranate peel extract, and simvastatin (a statin-based medication) that could offer a more effective way to treat periodontitis, a serious form of gum disease.

The team, from the Faculty of Medical and Health Sciences (FCMS) at the Pontifical Catholic University of São Paulo (PUC-SP) in Sorocaba, developed the material as part of an effort to improve treatment options for a condition that affects the tissues supporting the teeth.

Periodontitis is a chronic inflammatory disease caused by infection. Over time, it can destroy the structures that hold teeth in place, leading to bone loss and reduced attachment between teeth and surrounding tissues.

Current treatments are designed to control infection and inflammation, but they generally do little to regenerate damaged periodontal tissue. Other approaches, including guided tissue regeneration and bone grafting, have been explored, but their results can vary and are often difficult to predict.

Combining Natural Materials and Medicine

To overcome these limitations, the researchers investigated natural bioactive materials that could address several aspects of the disease at the same time.

"We began to view latex extracted from jackfruit as an interesting alternative, as it has adhesive properties. This led us to believe that it could remain longer at the site affected by periodontitis, promoting a more targeted release of therapeutic compounds and potentially reducing the need for systemic antibiotic use," explains Professor Eliana Aparecida de Rezende Duek, from the Department of Surgery at the FCMS.

Duek coordinated the study, which was supported by FAPESP (projects 23/17083-8 and 23/12039-0) and published in the journal Polymer Bulletin.

The researchers combined jackfruit latex with pomegranate peel extract, which is known for its antimicrobial properties when applied topically, and simvastatin, an anti-inflammatory drug that has also been studied for its ability to stimulate bone formation. Together, the ingredients formed a mucoadhesive matrix designed to act directly on damaged tissue.

Using simvastatin in this way may offer another advantage. When taken orally, most of the drug is retained by the liver, and only a small amount enters the bloodstream. As a result, higher doses are often needed, increasing the risk of side effects, including acute muscle degeneration. Delivering the drug directly to the affected area could help avoid that problem.

Testing the Jackfruit Latex Gel

To create the biomaterial, the scientists manually collected latex from freshly harvested jackfruit and carefully purified it. Pomegranate peel extract was then incorporated into the material.

The team carried out a series of physicochemical and biological analyses to better understand how the biomaterial was structured and how it behaved under laboratory conditions.

The researchers also performed an in vitro study using human adipose-derived stem cells. Simvastatin was added to the gel at concentrations of 0.3%, 0.6%, and 1.2%. These levels did not alter the gel's structure and were considered technically safe.

All three concentrations promoted osteoinduction, the process that encourages cells to develop into bone-forming tissue, within 14 days. The effect became even stronger after 21 days, supporting the material's potential as a treatment for periodontitis.

Encouraging Early Results

"Overall, the results were very encouraging for us. We observed that the developed biomaterial has great potential for future applications in treating periodontitis and in other areas as well, especially since it involves a material that has received little attention in the scientific literature for biomedical use," says Duek.

She cautions that additional research is still needed before the technology can move closer to clinical use.

"Despite these promising results, we're continuing to move forward with new studies to more thoroughly evaluate the efficacy and safety of the system," she adds.
articles.reasoning: The article reports on a promising new biomaterial developed from natural substances that could significantly improve treatment for periodontitis, a widespread and serious gum disease. The innovation combines natural and pharmaceutical components to not only combat infection and inflammation but also promote tissue and bone regeneration, addressing a major limitation of current treatments. The research is detailed, with early encouraging results and clear potential for broad health benefits, making it a meaningful advancement in dental health care.
articles.personality_title: New gel made from jackfruit and pomegranate shows promise for gum disease treatment
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Periodontitis is a serious gum disease that causes infection and inflammation, leading to loss of bone and tissue around teeth. Current treatments mainly control infection but do not rebuild the damaged tissue.

**What happened** – Researchers in Brazil developed a new gel using natural ingredients: jackfruit latex, pomegranate peel extract, and a medicine called simvastatin. This gel sticks to the gums and slowly releases the medicine to fight infection and help regrow bone and tissue. Early laboratory tests showed the gel helped stem cells turn into bone cells within 14 to 21 days.

**Impact** – This new gel could improve how periodontitis is treated by not only stopping infection but also helping to repair the damage. Unlike current treatments, it targets the problem directly and may reduce the need for high doses of medicine, which can cause side effects.

**What's next step** – The researchers will continue testing to make sure the gel is safe and effective before it can be used in clinics. More studies will explore how well it works in real patients.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A new natural gel combining jackfruit latex, pomegranate, and medicine shows early promise for healing severe gum disease by fighting infection and rebuilding damaged tissue.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un nouveau gel à base de jacquier et grenade promet un traitement pour la maladie des gencives
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La parodontite est une maladie grave des gencives qui provoque infection et inflammation, entraînant la perte d’os et de tissu autour des dents. Les traitements actuels contrôlent surtout l’infection sans reconstruire les tissus endommagés.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs brésiliens ont créé un nouveau gel utilisant des ingrédients naturels : latex de jacquier, extrait de peau de grenade, et un médicament appelé simvastatine. Ce gel adhère aux gencives et libère lentement le médicament pour combattre l’infection et aider à régénérer os et tissu. Des tests en laboratoire ont montré que le gel aidait les cellules à devenir des cellules osseuses en 14 à 21 jours.

**Impact** – Ce gel pourrait améliorer le traitement de la parodontite en stoppant l’infection et en réparant les dégâts. Contrairement aux traitements actuels, il agit directement sur le problème et pourrait réduire les doses de médicament, limitant ainsi les effets secondaires.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs vont poursuivre les tests pour vérifier la sécurité et l’efficacité du gel avant son utilisation en clinique. D’autres études testeront son efficacité chez les patients.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – Un nouveau gel naturel combinant latex de jacquier, grenade et médicament montre un potentiel pour guérir la parodontite en combattant l’infection et en reconstruisant les tissus endommagés.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevo gel de yaca y granada muestra promesa para tratar la enfermedad de las encías
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La periodontitis es una enfermedad grave de las encías que causa infección e inflamación, llevando a la pérdida de hueso y tejido alrededor de los dientes. Los tratamientos actuales controlan la infección pero no reconstruyen el tejido dañado.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores en Brasil desarrollaron un nuevo gel usando ingredientes naturales: látex de yaca, extracto de cáscara de granada y un medicamento llamado simvastatina. Este gel se adhiere a las encías y libera lentamente el medicamento para combatir la infección y ayudar a regenerar hueso y tejido. Pruebas de laboratorio mostraron que el gel ayudó a células madre a convertirse en células óseas en 14 a 21 días.

**Impacto** – Este gel nuevo podría mejorar el tratamiento de la periodontitis al detener la infección y reparar el daño. A diferencia de los tratamientos actuales, actúa directamente sobre el problema y podría reducir la necesidad de dosis altas de medicamento, evitando efectos secundarios.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores continuarán con pruebas para asegurar que el gel sea seguro y efectivo antes de usarlo en clínicas. Más estudios evaluarán su eficacia en pacientes reales.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Un nuevo gel natural que combina látex de yaca, granada y medicamento muestra potencial para curar la periodontitis al combatir la infección y reconstruir el tejido dañado.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a large, ripe jackfruit split open to reveal its golden latex flowing gently and merging with vibrant pomegranate peels beside it, forming a softly glowing, adhesive gel that envelops and repairs a stylized, abstract representation of healthy tooth roots and gums, all rendered in natural, earthy tones.

articles.title: Faecal transplant makes the brains of old mice act young again
articles.summary: Older mice that received a faecal microbiome transplant from younger animals went on to have improved brain plasticity, which suggests their brains could overcome a neurological condition that is typically successfully treated only in childhood
articles.content:
Older mice that received a faecal microbiome transplant from younger animals went on to have improved brain plasticity, which suggests their brains could overcome a neurological condition that is typically successfully treated only in childhood

A scanning electron micrograph of the intestinal lining of a mouse, with several bacteria (green) and one red blood cell (red) CJC Copyright: IKELOS GmbH/Dr. Christopher B. Jackson/Science Photo Library

A faecal microbiome transplant (FMT) could make an aged brain as adaptable as a young one. Our gut microbiome has been linked to our risk of depression and may even play a role in shaping our personality. But for the first time, a study has shown that older mice given the gut microbiomes of younger animals via an FMT experience improved brain plasticity. This suggests that they could overcome a condition similar to amblyopia, also known as lazy eye, which is typically only successfully treated in childhood.

“This study suggests that microbial communities may help regulate critical periods of brain development by defining when developmental windows of heightened plasticity open and close,” says Parisa Gazerani at Oslo Metropolitan University in Norway, who wasn’t involved in the work. “It suggests that the gut microbiome may be an active developmental partner that helps shape neural circuit maturation alongside sensory experience, immune activity and genetic programming.”

Neuroplasticity, the brain’s ability to remodel itself, means that conditions like amblyopia can be treated in children by temporarily covering their stronger eye. This forces the brain to forge new connections to the weaker eye, improving overall vision. But plasticity peaks at a young age, decreasing as our brains naturally prune unused connections during adolescence.

Paola Tognini at the Sant’Anna School of Advanced Studies in Pisa, Italy, and her colleagues wanted to see whether the gut microbiome is involved in this and could be manipulated to boost brain plasticity in adulthood.

First, they gave 21-day-old mice a high dose of broad-spectrum antibiotics dissolved in water every day for 10 days, and found substantial changes to their gut microbiomes compared with a control group of mice that had untreated water. This included reduced levels of bacterial families such as Lachnospiraceae, which is involved in making short-chain fatty acids with neuroprotective properties.

Free newsletter Sign up to Eight Weeks to a Healthier You Your science-backed guide to the easy habits that will help you sleep well, stress less, eat smarter and age better. Sign up to newsletter

Each mouse then had one eye sealed for three days. After this, when the researchers imaged the neural responses to the stimulation of each eye, they found that only the control mice showed evidence of neuroplasticity, with their brains responding more to stimulation of the eye that had stayed open.

To investigate what might be behind the change, the team did RNA sequencing to reveal which genes were switched on in the mice’s visual cortex. “We found dramatic alterations in the animals receiving the antibiotic cocktail,” says Tognini. More than 1000 genes were differently expressed in these mice compared with the control ones. These included genes related to the process of myelination, when nerves get wrapped in a protective sheath, and the permeability of the blood-brain barrier.

Finally, the team transplanted the faecal microbiota of mice aged around 30 days old into 4-month-old adult mice, while a control group received transplants from other adults. Only the brains of the mice receiving the young microbiota demonstrated neuroplasticity in response to the eye-shutting experiment.

If the same applies to people, the implications could be huge, says Harriët Schellekens at University College Cork in Ireland. “It would suggest that the microbiome is not only important for early-life brain development, but might also be targeted later in life to enhance learning, recovery after injury, or resilience in ageing and neurological disease,” she says. “The challenge will be to identify the specific microbial metabolites or strains responsible, rather than relying on crude microbiota transplants.”

However, direct extrapolation to people is premature, says Gazerani, primarily because our brains are more complex and our microbiomes are very influenced by our diet and lifestyles.

The study also raises questions about the potential long-term effects of early-life antibiotic exposure, says Gazerani, particularly if the dose is high and prolonged. “Although antibiotics remain lifesaving and should never be withheld when clinically indicated, these findings reinforce the importance of using them judiciously during critical developmental windows,” she says.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough showing that fecal microbiome transplants from young to old mice can restore brain plasticity, a key factor in neurological health and recovery. This finding has broad potential implications for treating neurological conditions and improving brain function in aging humans, representing a meaningful advance in health science with substantial real-world impact.
articles.personality_title: Gut bacteria from young mice help older mice’s brains stay flexible
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – As animals, including humans, age, their brains become less flexible. This flexibility, called brain plasticity, helps the brain learn new skills and recover from injuries. Usually, this plasticity is strongest when we are young and fades as we grow older.

**What happened** – Scientists gave older mice a faecal microbiome transplant (FMT), which means they took gut bacteria from young mice and put it into older mice. After this transplant, the older mice showed improved brain plasticity. The researchers tested this by temporarily covering one eye of the mice, a method used to study brain changes. Only the older mice that received young gut bacteria showed changes in their brain responses, similar to young mice.

**Impact** – This discovery shows for the first time that gut bacteria can make an older brain act more like a young one. It suggests that the gut microbiome, the community of bacteria living in the intestines, plays a role in how the brain develops and stays flexible. This could lead to new ways to help adults recover from brain injuries or improve brain function as they age.

**What's next step** – Researchers want to find out which specific bacteria or substances from the gut are responsible for these brain changes. They also need to test if similar effects happen in humans, since human brains and gut bacteria are more complex. This work could eventually lead to treatments that improve brain health in older people.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Transferring gut bacteria from young to old mice can restore brain flexibility, opening new paths to support brain health during aging.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les bactéries intestinales de jeunes souris rendent le cerveau des souris âgées plus flexible
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – En vieillissant, le cerveau devient moins flexible. Cette flexibilité, appelée plasticité cérébrale, aide le cerveau à apprendre et à se réparer. Elle est plus forte quand on est jeune et diminue avec l’âge.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des scientifiques ont transplanté le microbiome intestinal de jeunes souris chez des souris plus âgées. Après cette transplantation, les souris âgées ont montré une meilleure plasticité cérébrale. Les chercheurs ont testé cela en couvrant temporairement un œil des souris. Seules les souris âgées ayant reçu le microbiome des jeunes ont eu des changements dans leur cerveau, comme les jeunes souris.

**Impact** – Cette découverte montre pour la première fois que les bactéries intestinales peuvent rendre un cerveau âgé plus jeune. Cela suggère que le microbiome joue un rôle dans le développement et la flexibilité du cerveau. Cela pourrait permettre de nouvelles façons d’aider les adultes à récupérer après une blessure au cerveau ou à mieux fonctionner en vieillissant.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs veulent identifier quelles bactéries ou substances du microbiome causent ces changements. Ils doivent aussi vérifier si cela fonctionne chez l’humain, car notre cerveau et notre microbiome sont plus complexes. Ce travail pourrait mener à des traitements pour la santé du cerveau chez les personnes âgées.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Transférer les bactéries intestinales de jeunes chez des souris âgées peut restaurer la flexibilité cérébrale, ouvrant de nouvelles voies pour soutenir la santé du cerveau en vieillissant.
articles.personality_title_es: Las bacterias intestinales de ratones jóvenes hacen que el cerebro de ratones viejos sea más flexible
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – A medida que envejecemos, nuestro cerebro se vuelve menos flexible. Esta flexibilidad, llamada plasticidad cerebral, ayuda al cerebro a aprender y recuperarse. Es más fuerte cuando somos jóvenes y disminuye con la edad.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos transplantaron el microbioma intestinal de ratones jóvenes a ratones viejos. Después del trasplante, los ratones viejos mostraron una mejor plasticidad cerebral. Los investigadores lo probaron cubriendo un ojo temporalmente. Solo los ratones viejos que recibieron el microbioma joven mostraron cambios en el cerebro, como los ratones jóvenes.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento muestra por primera vez que las bacterias intestinales pueden hacer que un cerebro viejo actúe como uno joven. Esto sugiere que el microbioma intestinal ayuda a desarrollar y mantener la flexibilidad del cerebro. Podría abrir nuevas formas de ayudar a adultos a recuperarse de lesiones cerebrales o mejorar la función cerebral al envejecer.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos quieren saber qué bacterias o sustancias del microbioma causan estos cambios. También deben probar si esto funciona en humanos, ya que nuestro cerebro y microbioma son más complejos. Este trabajo podría llevar a tratamientos para la salud cerebral en personas mayores.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Trasplantar bacterias intestinales de ratones jóvenes a ratones viejos puede restaurar la plasticidad cerebral, abriendo nuevas posibilidades para apoyar la salud del cerebro con la edad.
articles.image_prompt: An intricate, softly colored painting of two mice—one older with subtle gray fur, the other younger with vibrant fur—connected by a glowing, flowing ribbon of diverse, luminous gut bacteria shaped like tiny green and blue microbes traveling from the young to the old mouse, set against a warm, abstract backdrop that hints at neural networks and brain synapses gently lighting up.
